perturbation.original_text: In the event either party discloses, disseminates or releases any Proprietary Information received from the other party or threatens to do so, except as authorized hereunder, such disclosure, dissemination or release will be deemed a material breach of this Agreement and the disclosing party may demand prompt return of all Proprietary Information previously provided to such party and obtain a preliminary and permanent injunction enjoining any such disclosure, dissemination or release. The provisions of this paragraph are in addition to any other legal right or remedies the party whose Proprietary Information has been disclosed, disseminated or released may have under federal or state law including without limitation, any claims for disclosing party's direct and consequential damages. The prevailing party in any dispute hereunder shall be entitled to its costs and expenses in connection with enforcement and any claim for damages for any violation of this Agreement, including its reasonable attorneys' fees and court costs.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event either party discloses, disseminates or releases any Proprietary Information received from the other party or threatens to do so, except as authorized hereunder, such disclosure, dissemination or release will be deemed a material breach of this Agreement and the disclosing party may demand prompt return of all Proprietary Information previously provided to such party and obtain a preliminary and permanent injunction enjoining any such disclosure, dissemination or release. The provisions of this paragraph are in addition to any other legal right or remedies the party whose Proprietary Information has been disclosed, disseminated or released may have under federal or state law including without limitation, any claims for disclosing party's direct and consequential damages. The prevailing party in any dispute hereunder shall be entitled to its costs and expenses in connection with enforcement and any claim for damages for any violation of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Removing 'including its reasonable attorneys' fees and court costs' limits the remedies available to the prevailing party, potentially contradicting Nevada law regarding the recovery of attorney's fees in breach of contract cases. This creates a legal contradiction by omitting a standard legal remedy.
perturbation.law_explanation: NRS 18.010 allows for the court to award reasonable attorney's fees to the prevailing party in certain contract actions. By omitting the mention of attorney's fees, the contract suggests attorney's fees are not recoverable, which may contradict the statute's provisions allowing such recovery at the court's discretion, especially if the court finds that the defense was brought without reasonable grounds or to harass the plaintiff.

perturbation.justification: The modified contract omits 'including its reasonable attorneys' fees and court costs,' limiting the prevailing party's remedies. The Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation state that this omission potentially contradicts Nevada law regarding attorney's fees in breach of contract cases, because NRS 18.010 allows for the court to award reasonable attorney's fees. Scraped Law Snippet 1, under 'NRS 18.010 Award of attorney’s fees', corroborates this. The omission creates a legal contradiction because it removes a remedy that might otherwise be available under Nevada law.

perturbation.original_text: ADC and Polaroid agree to make full disclosure of any business dealings or arrangements with third parties, persons, or entities introduced by the other party in connection with such Confidential Information and/or projects. The spirit of mutual trust and confidence and equitable treatment, shall be the underlying principle of this undertaking, and ADC and Polaroid agree to adhere thereto.
perturbation.changed_text: ADC and Polaroid agree to disclose any business dealings or arrangements with third parties, persons, or entities introduced by the other party in connection with such Confidential Information and/or projects.
perturbation.explanation: Removing 'The spirit of mutual trust and confidence and equitable treatment, shall be the underlying principle of this undertaking, and ADC and Polaroid agree to adhere thereto.' weakens the obligation to act in good faith, potentially contradicting the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing present in Nevada contracts. This omission could be seen as a legal contradiction because it eliminates a critical principle guiding the parties' conduct.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Nevada law recognizes the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing in contracts.
perturbation.law_explanation: Nevada law recognizes the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing in every contract, requiring parties to act honestly and fairly. By removing the explicit statement about 'mutual trust and confidence and equitable treatment,' the contract weakens this implied duty, creating a possible contradiction if a party acts in bad faith, and the omission suggests such behavior is permissible, which it is not under Nevada law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-305. Open Price Term. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-305. Open Price Term. (1) The parties if they so intend can conclude a contract for sale even though the price is not settled. In such a case the price is a reasonable price at the time for delivery if (a) nothing is said as to price; or (b) the price is left to be agreed by the parties and they fail to agree; or (c) the price is to be fixed in terms of some agreed market or other standard as set or recorded by a third person or agency and it is not so set or recorded. (2) A price to be fixed by the seller or by the buyer means a price for him to fix in good faith . (3) When a price left to be fixed otherwise than by agreement of the parties fails to be fixed through fault of one party the other may at his option treat the contract as cancelled or himself fix a reasonable price. (4) Where, however, the parties intend not to be bound unless the price be fixed or agreed and it is not fixed or agreed there is no contract . In such a case the buyer must return any goods already received or if unable so to do must pay their reasonable value at the time of delivery and the seller must return any portion of the price paid on account. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The original contract text explicitly states that 'The spirit of mutual trust and confidence and equitable treatment, shall be the underlying principle of this undertaking, and ADC and Polaroid agree to adhere thereto.' The changed text removes this statement, weakening the obligation to act in good faith. While Scraped Law Snippet 2 is empty, the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation both highlight that Nevada law recognizes an implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing in every contract. Although Scraped Law Snippet 1 discusses 'good faith' only in the context of price fixing, the removal of the explicit statement promoting good faith weakens the contractual commitment and may violate the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing, as explained in the Law Explanation. The changed text, by omitting the original commitment to mutual trust, could be interpreted as permitting actions that are not in good faith, creating a contradiction of this implied covenant.

perturbation.original_text: This agreement shall be effective, covering all present and future negotiations by and between ADC and Polaroid concerning the use by ADC and Polaroid of such Confidential Information and/or projects from the date of this Agreement and shall continue for three (3) years. If ADC and Polaroid enter into any collateral agreements during this three year period, the provisions of this Non-disclosure and Non-circumvent agreement shall remain effective and in force until the expiration of any such subsequent or collateral agreements.
perturbation.changed_text: This agreement shall be effective, covering all present and future negotiations by and between ADC and Polaroid concerning the use by ADC and Polaroid of such Confidential Information and/or projects from the date of this Agreement and shall continue for three (3) years.
perturbation.explanation: Removing 'If ADC and Polaroid enter into any collateral agreements during this three year period, the provisions of this Non-disclosure and Non-circumvent agreement shall remain effective and in force until the expiration of any such subsequent or collateral agreements.' limits the duration of the agreement's effect, which may affect claims depending on the statute of limitations under Nevada law. In Nevada, the statute of limitations for breach of contract is typically six years for written contracts. The change could result in legal risk.
perturbation.law_explanation: NRS 11.190 specifies a six-year statute of limitations for actions based on written contracts. By removing the clause that extends the agreement's effect beyond the initial three years if collateral agreements exist, the contract could inadvertently shorten the period during which a party can bring a claim for breach related to those collateral agreements, potentially contradicting the statutory six-year limit.

perturbation.justification: The original contract text includes a clause that extends the non-disclosure agreement beyond the initial three years if there are any collateral agreements. The changed text removes this clause, limiting the agreement to only three years, regardless of any collateral agreements. The Law Explanation states that NRS 11.190 specifies a six-year statute of limitations for written contracts, and the change could shorten the period during which a party can bring a claim for breach related to those collateral agreements, potentially contradicting the statutory six-year limit. Scraped Law Snippet 2 contains 'NRS 11.190 Periods of limitation', which confirms the existence of laws in Nevada that limit the time one has to bring a claim. Therefore, the omission of the clause extending the agreement introduces a contradiction because it could shorten the legally mandated statute of limitations.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement constitutes the entire agreement between the parties and supersedes any prior or contemporaneous oral or written agreements and representations with regard to the subject matter thereof. This Agreement may not be modified except by writing signed by both parties. If any provision of this Agreement shall be invalid or unenforceable, the remainder of this Agreement shall remain in full force and effect.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement constitutes the entire agreement between the parties and supersedes any prior or contemporaneous oral or written agreements and representations with regard to the subject matter thereof. This Agreement may not be modified except by writing signed by both parties.
perturbation.explanation: Removing 'If any provision of this Agreement shall be invalid or unenforceable, the remainder of this Agreement shall remain in full force and effect.' eliminates the severability clause, potentially causing the entire agreement to be deemed unenforceable if any single provision is found invalid under Nevada law. This introduces legal risk because it removes a standard protection that ensures the rest of the contract remains valid even if a part is not.
perturbation.contradicted_law: General principles of contract law regarding severability in Nevada.
perturbation.law_explanation: While there isn't a specific Nevada statute mandating severability, Nevada courts generally follow the principles outlined in the Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 184, which supports severability unless the invalid provision is an essential part of the agreement. By removing the severability clause, the contract now implies that any invalid provision will void the entire agreement, which contradicts the default legal principle that allows courts to sever the invalid portion and enforce the rest of the contract.

Perhaps you wish to explore one of these other options? About Us Get Involved Events Community Publications Projects & Tools Last updated Mar 27, 2024
perturbation.justification: The original contract includes a severability clause, a standard protection that ensures the rest of the contract remains valid even if a part is not. The changed text removes this clause. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 is missing and Scraped Law Snippet 2 is a 404 error, the Law Explanation asserts that Nevada courts generally follow the principles outlined in the Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 184, which supports severability unless the invalid provision is an essential part of the agreement. By removing the severability clause, the contract implies that any invalid provision will void the entire agreement, which contradicts the default legal principle. This makes the contract non-compliant and creates a legal contradiction, as it removes a protection typically afforded under Nevada law. The removal significantly alters the enforceability of the contract.
